items.title: Directed weighted graph
items.caption: Vertices A–E, weighted directed edges. Shortest path from A to D is A → C → B → D (cost 1 + 2 + 3 = 6).

items.title: Adjacency list vs adjacency matrix
items.mermaid:
flowchart LR subgraph List["adjacency list (sparse)"] direction TB L1["A: [(B,4), (C,1)]"] L2["B: [(D,3)]"] L3["C: [(B,2), (D,5)]"] L4["D: [(E,1)]"] L5["E: []"] end subgraph Matrix["adjacency matrix (dense)"] direction TB M[" A B C D E
A . 4 1 . .
B . . . 3 .
C . 2 . 5 .
D . . . . 1
E . . . . ."] end
items.caption: List uses O(V+E) memory; matrix uses O(V²). List wins for sparse graphs (most knowledge graphs).

items.title: BFS vs DFS traversal orders
items.mermaid:
flowchart TB subgraph G["Graph (start = A)"] A((A)) --> B((B)) A --> C((C)) B --> D((D)) B --> E((E)) C --> F((F)) end Note["BFS (queue): A, B, C, D, E, F — level-by-level
DFS (stack): A, B, D, E, C, F — dive deep first"]
items.caption: Same algorithm skeleton, different worklist ADT — queue gives BFS, stack gives DFS.
